in affluent social settings, the person who asks great questions is almost always more compelling than the person who has all the answers. Because expertise is common in these rooms. In this chapter, what does **Cocktail attire** mean?

options.text: The affluent woman's wardrobe is built on a few exceptional pieces rather than many good ones. A quality blazer (The Row, Max Mara, Stella McCartney), a cashmere knit, a structured handbag, and well-made shoes create the foundation.
Dark suit (no tux) for men. Knee-length or midi dress, dressy separates, or a sharp jumpsuit for women. This is the most common dress code at galas, receptions, and evening events.

Women are often given less specific dress codes than men (“cocktail attire” means something very different from “black tie”, but both are vague). When in doubt, slightly overdressed is always better than slightly underdressed. And when the code says “casual,” it never means casual.
The aesthetic philosophy that wealth is signaled through quality, fabric, and fit rather than logos or visible branding. Also called “stealth wealth.”

questions.explanation: Dark suit (no tux) for men. Knee-length or midi dress, dressy separates, or a sharp jumpsuit for women. This is the most common dress code at galas, receptions, and evening events.

In this chapter, what does **This chapter gives you the map** mean?

options.text: the hotel hierarchy that determines where you stay, the destinations you should recognize by name, and the unspoken codes of how affluent people plan, experience, and talk about travel.

Aspen (the social ski destination—as much about the scene as the slopes), St. Moritz (old European money), Courchevel (the luxury French Alps—where Russian oligarchs built chalets), Whistler (accessible luxury, increasingly popular), Niseko (Japan's powder paradise).

The Serengeti and Masai Mara (the classic safari—Tanzania and Kenya), the Okavango Delta (Botswana—the luxury safari connoisseur's choice), Cape Town (wine, food, mountains, and culture—South Africa's jewel), Rwanda (gorilla trekking—the most transformative travel experience many affluent travelers report).
Beyond the Resort

In this chapter, what does **Par** mean?

options.text: Birdie (one under par), eagle (two under), bogey (one over), double bogey (two over). For a beginner, bogey golf is respectable. Don't worry about birdies.
The Unspoken Rules
A method for learning by asking questions instead of pretending.
The expected number of strokes to complete a hole. Par 3 (short), par 4 (medium), par 5 (long). A typical 18-hole course is par 70–72.

questions.prompt: In this chapter, what does **Weekly community** mean?

options.text: Whether it's church, mosque, synagogue, temple, or a meditation group—protect one gathering per week. The community aspect of spiritual life is irreplaceable. It's where you're known, where you serve, where your success doesn't matter as much as your presence.

options.text: A handwritten thank-you note is the most underutilized power tool in professional life. It costs $2 and creates more goodwill than a $200 gift. Send it within 48 hours.

A technique for ordering wine by choosing the cheapest bottle.

Open something good—this is not the time for the cheapest bottle on the shelf. Two to three bottles for six guests is standard. Having a specific story about the wine (“We picked this up in Napa last fall”) elevates the experience.

questions.prompt: Short answer: Host a dinner party in the next 60 days. Start small: 6 people, a simple menu, a good bottle of wine, and a set table. The first one is the hardest. By the third, you’ll have a reputation as someone who brings people together—and that reputation compounds.

options.text: Asking someone directly what they earn, what they paid for something, or what their net worth is remains deeply taboo. Even close friends rarely have this conversation. The exception: real estate purchase prices, which are public record and therefore fair game.

Not stock tips—macro themes. “How are you thinking about interest rates?” “What are you seeing in your industry?” Having one or two informed opinions about the current economic moment (read one article per week from the Financial Times or Bloomberg) is sufficient.

Divisive politics (unless you know the room), religion in a preachy mode (Chapter 19 covers the nuanced approach), gossip about mutual acquaintances, complaints about wealth (“taxes are killing me” reads poorly), and conspicuous humblebrags.

The Psychology of Money (Morgan Housel), Sapiens (Yuval Harari), Thinking, Fast and Slow (Daniel Kahneman), Shoe Dog (Phil Knight), Atomic Habits (James Clear). These circulate through affluent professional culture like currency.

options.text: A technique for ordering wine by choosing the cheapest bottle.

Take your children to the places and experiences in this book. The restaurant, the gallery, the golf course, the travel destination. Don't shield them from your new world—include them in it. A child who visits a museum at six is building cultural capital that compounds for decades.

This is the hard part. The fact that you can afford something doesn't mean your child should automatically have it. Chores, earned privileges, delayed gratification, and the word “no” are not deprivations—they're gifts. The most dangerous thing you can give a child is the sense that comfort is guaranteed.
A tier used to describe net worth and lifestyle differences.

questions.prompt: Short answer: Start one family financial conversation this month, calibrated to your children’s age. For young kids: a savings jar and a chore chart. For teens: a real budget conversation. For young adults: an honest discussion about the family’s values around money. The conversation itself is the gift.

In this chapter, what does **Body** mean?

options.text: achievement is external. It's measured by others—the title, the income, the possessions. Joy is internal. It's measured by you—the quality of your morning, the depth of your relationships, the alignment between your values and your life. Achievement can be taken away. Joy, once cultivated, is resilient.

your credentials, your expertise, your reputation, your earning power. This is the wealth that got you into this book. It's essential, but it's also the most fragile—one industry disruption, one health crisis, one burnout episode can destabilize it.

the depth and breadth of your relationships, the people who would take your call at midnight, the network that generates opportunities, advice, and support. Every dinner party you host, every round of golf you play, every benefit you attend is an investment in relational wealth. It compounds more reliably than any stock.
Am I physically healthy? Am I training consistently? Am I sleeping well? Is my energy where it needs to be?

questions.prompt: Short answer: Schedule your first three-pillar check-in right now. Put it on your calendar for 90 days from today. Ask yourself the nine questions above. Adjust accordingly. Then schedule the next one. This simple practice is the operating system for everything else in this book.
In this chapter, what does **Month 4** mean?

options.text: Host & Connect. Host a second dinner party—more ambitious this time (better wine, a theme, or a private chef). Attend a benefit dinner and work the room using the Chapter 24 strategies. Strengthen one key professional relationship through shared experience (golf, dinner, travel).

Social & Spiritual. Host your first dinner party (6–8 people, simple menu, good wine). Attend one cultural event (gallery opening, benefit dinner, charity gala). Reconnect with your faith community if lapsed—attend one service or gathering. Send three handwritten thank-you notes this month.

Art & Culture. Visit a gallery or museum. Attend an auction preview at Christie's, Sotheby's, or Phillips (free, open to public). Buy your first piece of art ($500–2,000—emerging artist or print). Read one book from the Chapter 25 reading list.

Investment & Board. Meet with a wealth advisor. Explore board service opportunities at nonprofits you care about. Deepen your wine knowledge: take a class, visit a region, or join a tasting group. Begin building your 12-bottle collection.
